Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows: (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

CROSS DEFENDANT W. BRADLEY ELECTRIC, INC.'S Motion For An Order Compelling Vanessa Whirl To Appear For Deposition, Etc.

Bradley Electric, Inc.'s motion for an order compelling attorney Vanessa Whirl's deposition is granted.

In this wrongful death case, Mitchell Engineering cross-claimed against Bradley and DeHaro-Rameriz Corporation. On January 5, 2022, Mitchell attorney Whirl signed and filed a voluntary dismissal with prejudice of Mitchell's second amended cross-complaint. Mitchell's owner and president Curtis Mitchell insists he "never consented or provided any authority, to anyone, including Vanessa Whirl" for the cross-complaint's dismissal. (7/1/22 Mitchell Memo. 4:8-10; see also Mitchell Dec. 4:3-4.) On July 1, Mitchell filed a motion to vacate and set aside the dismissal on grounds of mistake, surprise and/or excusable neglect. (CCP 473.)
I granted 60 days for discovery regarding Mitchell's motion, and Whirl's deposition was noticed. Mitchell's new lawyers object to the deposition on attorney-client privilege grounds.

Communications about the dismissal between attorney Whirl and client Curtis Mitchell would likely be crucial evidence regarding Mitchell's motion to vacate or set aside that dismissal. "When a party asserting a claim invokes privilege to withhold crucial evidence, the policy favoring full disclosure of relevant evidence conflicts with the policy underlying the privilege. Courts have resolved this conflict by holding that the proponent of the claim must give up the privilege in order to pursue the claim. Where privileged information goes to the heart of the claim, fundamental fairness requires that it be disclosed for the litigation to proceed." (Steiny & Co., Inc. v. California Electric Supply Co. (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 285, 292.)

The hearing date on Mitchell's pending motion is continued from October 6 to November 10. Supplemental briefs shall be filed by November 3.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Victoria Thomas CSR#12927, 559-623-3367, vlthomascsr@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

Atty. IEENOR W. LIN, Esq. of Borton Petrini, LLP (Tel: 408-535-0870) for defendant Mebrhatu Berhe Brhane.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Motion To Seal.

The Court notes it did not receive from any party a notice of intent to contest the court's tentative ruling. Appearing counsel indicate they are not contesting the court's tentative ruling. Matter submitted without argument.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc.'s Motion to Seal Documents is granted pursuant to California Rules of Court, rule 2.550 subdivision (c). The adduced documents contain financially sensitive information. (Disharoon Dec., 5.) Confidential financial information is the proper subject of a motion to seal. (See Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 1273, 1286.) Therefore, the Court finds that (1) there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the records, (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records, (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the records are not sealed, (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored and (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest.

Order signed.

Plaintiffs' motion for determination of good faith settlement is granted.

The settlement satisfies the factors of CCP 877.6 and Tech-Bilt v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488: (1) the decedent and non-settler Mitchell Engineering both appear to have major shares of the proportionate liability; (2) a significant amount is being paid by the settling defendants; (3) the plaintiffs - decedent's four adult children - do not disagree re settlement allocation; (4) settlers should pay less in settlement than they would after trial (but based on my experience in San Francisco wrongful death cases, it is not significantly less); (5) settlers' insurance is paying policy limits; (6) there is no indication of collusion or fraud; rather, plaintiffs and settlers are both represented by highly respected and skilled counsel.
In sum, Mitchell has not carried its burden of showing that the settlement is "out of the ballpark." (Tech-Bilt, 38 Cal.3d at 499-500.)

Order signed.
